benchmark_annotation.summary: Blank lines through QR code when trying to set up MFA

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Enrollment / Client Rendering Issue
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
Mac terminal rendering can distort SSH QR code during MFA enrollment. Enrollment can still proceed by scanning if possible, or manually entering the secret key in authenticator app.

ACTION
1) Confirm issue is known for some Mac terminal setups.
2) Provide manual secret-key fallback method.
3) Clarify expected token labels for SSH vs web.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi James,

This is a known issue on some Mac setups where the QR may render poorly. You can still complete setup by manually entering the secret key in your authenticator app if scanning fails.

Please follow:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html

The SSH token should appear as `CSD3: SSH Login` (distinct from login-web token naming).

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not reset MFA unnecessarily when manual enrollment path is available.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA / SSH Connection
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with MFA when trying to connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk via SSH, with the QR code being replaced by garbage or broken up by blank lines [1][2]. This is a known issue that may require a reset of the MFA secret [2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, we will need to reset the MFA secret for the user. This will require a quick video call to verify the user's photo ID [4]. Once the ID is verified, we can reset the MFA secret and provide the user with a new QR code [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please confirm your username and the exact error message you are seeing when trying to connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk via SSH?
- Have you tried manually entering the code from the authenticator app, as described in the documentation [2]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
We can assist you with resetting your MFA secret. To do so, we will need to have a quick video call to verify your photo ID. Please let us know a time and date that is suitable for you, and we will send an MS Teams meeting invite.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- MFA resets require verification of the user's photo ID to ensure security and compliance [4].
- Users should be instructed to manually enter the code from the authenticator app if the QR code is not working [2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-54226
[2] : HPCSSUP-94321
[4] : HPCSSUP-73968

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Cloud VM Configuration / GPU Containers / MPI
Technical Action Required: No (guidance)
Escalation Required: Optional (if capacity constrained)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User needed guidance for CUDA+MPI in Azimuth GPU VM. Recommended approach: install CUDA toolkit and Nvidia driver in VM, prefer Singularity for HPC container runtime with GPU bindings, and choose MPI runtime inside container as needed. Capacity caveat: GPU pool may be fully utilised.

ACTION
1) Confirm current GPU capacity availability.
2) Advise VM-level CUDA/driver install for GPU instances.
3) Recommend Singularity/Apptainer over Docker for HPC runtime.
4) Explain MPI runtime can be selected within container image.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bradley,

On Azimuth GPU VMs, you can install the CUDA toolkit and NVIDIA open-source driver in the usual way. For HPC workflows we generally recommend Singularity/Apptainer rather than Docker, including GPU bindings to expose host GPUs.

MPI runtime selection is up to your container build choice.

Please note GPU capacity may currently be fully allocated, so provisioning can be constrained.

Thanks,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply immediate GPU availability when capacity is saturated.

benchmark_annotation.summary: Activating Conda Environment in Jupyter Notebook (Login-Web Interface)

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Jupyter / Login-Web / User Environment
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User could not select a conda environment kernel in Login-Web Jupyter. Resolution is to ensure the environment has `ipykernel` and is explicitly registered as a Jupyter kernel, then restart/reopen the notebook session.

ACTION
1) Confirm environment prerequisites
   - Environment exists and can be activated.
   - `ipykernel` is installed in that environment.
2) Register the environment as a Jupyter kernel
   - Activate env and run:
     `python -m ipykernel install --user --name <env_name> --display-name "Python (<env_name>)"`
3) Ask user to restart/reopen Login-Web Jupyter session and select the new kernel.
4) If kernel still does not appear, collect diagnostics:
   - `jupyter kernelspec list`
   - exact env activation/install commands used.
5) Share official docs for full workflow/reference.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Patrick,

Please register your conda environment as a Jupyter kernel, then restart/reopen your Login-Web Jupyter session.

Example:

```bash
conda activate <env_name>
python -m ipykernel install --user --name <env_name> --display-name "Python (<env_name>)"
```

After that, select `Python (<env_name>)` from the Jupyter kernel menu.

Full guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#adding-virtual-environments

If it still doesn’t appear, please share:
- `jupyter kernelspec list`
- the exact commands you used to create and register the environment.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Keep instructions aligned with the documented Login-Web kernel registration workflow.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Jupyter Notebook / Conda Environment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
To activate a Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook, the user needs to install the ipykernel package in the Conda environment and then install the kernel in Jupyter [1]. The user has already created a Conda environment with ipykernel installed but is unable to switch the kernel to the Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, the user should run the following commands in the terminal:
1. `pip install ipykernel` (if not already installed) [1]
2. `ipython kernel install --user --name=[conda_venv_name]` (replace [conda_venv_name] with the actual name of the Conda environment) [1]
Then, in the Jupyter Notebook, the user should be able to select the Conda environment as a kernel.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm that you have installed the ipykernel package in your Conda environment?
- What is the exact name of your Conda environment?
- Are you using the Login-Web interface to access Jupyter Notebook? If so, please note that it may not be possible to modify the environment within the interface [2].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To activate your Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook, please ensure that you have installed the ipykernel package and installed the kernel as described above. If you are still experiencing issues, please provide more details about your environment and the exact error messages you are seeing.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always install the ipykernel package in the Conda environment to enable kernel installation in Jupyter [1].
- Be aware of the limitations of the Login-Web interface when using Jupyter Notebook [2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-87352
[2] : HPCSSUP-52089

The user has already created a Conda environment with ipykernel installed but is unable to switch the kernel to the Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, the user should run the following commands in the terminal:
1. `pip install ipykernel` (if not already installed) [1]
2. `ipython kernel install --user --name=[conda_venv_name]` (replace [conda_venv_name] with the actual name of the Conda environment) [1]
Then, in the Jupyter Notebook, the user should be able to select the Conda environment as a kernel.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm that you have installed the ipykernel package in your Conda environment?
- What is the exact name of your Conda environment?
- Are you using the Login-Web interface to access Jupyter Notebook? If so, please note that it may not be possible to modify the environment within the interface [2].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To activate your Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook, please ensure that you have installed the ipykernel package and installed the kernel as described above. If you are still experiencing issues, please provide more details about your environment and the exact error messages you are seeing.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always install the ipykernel package in the Conda environment to enable kernel installation in Jupyter [1].
- Be aware of the limitations of the Login-Web interface when using Jupyter Notebook [2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-87352
[2] : HPCSSUP-52089

benchmark_annotation.summary: Unable to install packages for R in Citrix Workspace

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP / Package Management / Restricted Network
Technical Action Required: Yes (staff-managed package availability)
Escalation Required: Yes (SRCP team)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on SRCP RStudio could not install CRAN packages due to no external internet by design. SRCP policy requires curated package availability managed by staff; users may need to use internal repo mirror and request missing packages.

ACTION
1) Explain SRCP security model (no direct external repo access).
2) Escalate to SRCP team for package provisioning.
3) Ask user for package list.
4) Provide internal repo usage (`options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")`).
5) Track/fork to dedicated package request ticket if needed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Yira,

On SRCP, direct access to external package repositories is restricted by design. Please use the internal R repo:

options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")
available.packages()

If required packages are still missing, please send the exact package list and we can add them through the SRCP process.

Regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not suggest bypassing SRCP network restrictions.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RDS Farm / Package Installation [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Escalation Required: Possibly [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with installing R packages (e.g., dplyr, tidyverse) in RStudio via RDS Farm, with an error message indicating "error reading from connection" [1]. This suggests that the user may not be using the internal SRCP repository, which is required for package installation [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm that the user has set the internal SRCP repository in their R configuration using the command: `options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")` [1].
2. If the user has not set the internal repository, provide instructions on how to do so and request that they try installing the packages again [1].
3. If the issue persists, request that the user provide more details about the error message and the steps they took to install the packages [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide the exact error message you are seeing when trying to install the packages?
- Have you tried setting the internal SRCP repository in your R configuration using the command: `options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")` [1]?
- Can you please provide more details about the steps you took to install the packages?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your email. I have tried setting the internal SRCP repository in my R configuration using the command: `options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")` [1]. However, I am still experiencing issues with installing the packages. Please find the exact error message below.
Best regards,
Yira

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The internal SRCP repository must be used for package installation in RStudio via RDS Farm, as access to the internet is blocked [1].
- Users must set the internal repository in their R configuration using the command: `options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")` [1].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-88353

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Account Entitlements / CPU Access
Technical Action Required: Yes (entitlement check)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on a Tier2 GPU project requested CPU-only runs on icelake. The correct response is to first verify current CPU account entitlements (e.g. via `mybalance`) before advising which account to use.

ACTION
1) Verify user’s current account entitlements (`mybalance` or equivalent account view).
2) If an SL3 CPU account is present, instruct user to submit CPU jobs with that account.
3) If no CPU account is present, provide the correct request/provisioning path.
4) Clarify SL2 path for larger/longer CPU requirements where applicable.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi James,

Please check your available project accounts with:
`mybalance`

If you see an SL3 CPU account there, you can use that for CPU-only runs on the relevant CPU partition.

If you need larger/longer CPU resources than SL3 allows (or no CPU account is listed), please request the appropriate SL2 CPU access via your group lead.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Confirm actual entitlement list before advising new provisioning.
metrics.user_input:
Dear HPC support,
I am a PhD student with Pietro Lio project cs181 which has access to the Ampere node partition.
I have a need for some CPU only runs on the icelake nodes and was wondering if there's anyway to get this?
Many thanks
James Rowbottom - jr908
________________________________
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 09 December 2024 17:25
To: James Rowbottom <jr908@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: New Tier2 Account on CSD3 (jr908)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Before you login for the first time, please use your SSH
public key to login via SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
Once MFA is configured, please use your SSH public key to
login via SSH to
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge
Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your
project's scratch directory (rds-t2-cs181). Please note that
this is shared per project so each user will probably wish to create
their own subdirectory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjr908%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cb2a72e3346134cb8757b08dd187683f7%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638693619533696586%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=GUPkCeyukD4EZY8jc2oXOIpFpIpHahjHvOyx2dOSqx4%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjr908%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cb2a72e3346134cb8757b08dd187683f7%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638693619533696586%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=GUPkCeyukD4EZY8jc2oXOIpFpIpHahjHvOyx2dOSqx4%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The project listed by mybalance is the project you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A t2-cs181-XXX
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A t2-cs181-XXX ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for DiRAC jobs are currently:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

The scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge
Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your
project's scratch directory (rds-t2-cs181). Please note that
this is shared per project so each user will probably wish to create
their own subdirectory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjr908%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cb2a72e3346134cb8757b08dd187683f7%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638693619533696586%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=GUPkCeyukD4EZY8jc2oXOIpFpIpHahjHvOyx2dOSqx4%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjr908%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cb2a72e3346134cb8757b08dd187683f7%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638693619533696586%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=GUPkCeyukD4EZY8jc2oXOIpFpIpHahjHvOyx2dOSqx4%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The project listed by mybalance is the project you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A t2-cs181-XXX
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A t2-cs181-XXX ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for DiRAC jobs are currently:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Account Entitlements / CPU Access
Technical Action Required: Yes (entitlement check)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on a Tier2 GPU project requested CPU-only runs on icelake. The correct response is to first verify current CPU account entitlements (e.g. via `mybalance`) before advising which account to use.

ACTION
1) Verify user’s current account entitlements (`mybalance` or equivalent account view).
2) If an SL3 CPU account is present, instruct user to submit CPU jobs with that account.
3) If no CPU account is present, provide the correct request/provisioning path.
4) Clarify SL2 path for larger/longer CPU requirements where applicable.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi James,

Please check your available project accounts with:
`mybalance`

If you see an SL3 CPU account there, you can use that for CPU-only runs on the relevant CPU partition.

If you need larger/longer CPU resources than SL3 allows (or no CPU account is listed), please request the appropriate SL2 CPU access via your group lead.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Confirm actual entitlement list before advising new provisioning.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / Web Portal Login (Dirac)
Technical Action Required: No (credential workflow guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User unable to log into web portal despite SAFE access. Correct flow: use SAFE-provided password and web TOTP (`CSD3:<userid>`). If login flow appears inconsistent, refresh/reset SAFE password and retry.

ACTION
1) Confirm user ID and expected credential pair (SAFE password + web token label).
2) If not prompted correctly or repeated failure, request password reset through SAFE.
3) Confirm successful login after reset.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Karthika,

Please use the SAFE-provided password together with the web token labelled `CSD3:dc-bhuv1`.

If this still fails, please request a new password through SAFE and retry.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Distinguish web TOTP label from SSH token label.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login / Authentication Issue [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Escalation Required: Possibly [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user, Karthika, is experiencing difficulties logging into the Dirac machine, specifically with accessing the web portal [1]. The user has completed initial setup steps and obtained a QR code from the web portal token but is unable to log in [1]. A similar issue was previously reported and resolved in ticket HPCSSUP-80244 [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Request the user to try refreshing the page and attempting to log in again, as this previously resolved the issue [1].
2. If the issue persists, ask the user to request a new password through the SAFE account and follow the instructions for setting up MFA for SSH access [2].
3. Direct the user to the documentation for setting up MFA for SSH access: https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html [2].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide a screenshot of the error message you are receiving when attempting to log in?
- Have you tried logging in through a different method, such as MobaXterm or a local terminal?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Karthika,
Thank you for reaching out to us about your login issue. Could you please try refreshing the page and attempting to log in again? If the issue persists, please request a new password through the SAFE account and follow the instructions for setting up MFA for SSH access. Please find the documentation for setting up MFA for SSH access here: https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not provide passwords or sensitive information via email or ticket [1].
- Always direct users to official documentation and support channels [2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-80244
[2] : HPCSSUP-56186

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User asked about Slurm native `--checkpoint` functionality. Platform does not provide native Slurm checkpoint plugin support; users must implement checkpointing at application/workflow level. `dmtcp` module exists but compatibility with AlphaFold is user-dependent.

ACTION
1) Clarify no native Slurm checkpoint plugin on this cluster.
2) Recommend user-level checkpoint/restart implementation.
3) Mention `dmtcp` as optional tool to evaluate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Lisa,

Native Slurm checkpoint/restart is not enabled on this cluster, so `--checkpoint` options are not available here.

Checkpointing must be implemented at application/workflow level. We do provide a `dmtcp` module, though compatibility with AlphaFold would need to be tested in your workflow.

Kind regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply Slurm-native checkpoint flags are supported when plugin is absent.

Category: Checkpointing / Job Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to use checkpoints with a Slurm script for AlphaFold 2.3.2, but the --checkpoint command does not work [1]. The user has found documentation on checkpointing with Slurm, but it appears to be deprecated [1]. The Slurm version update log states that the checkpoint plugin interface and associated API calls have been removed [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
The user should consider using DMTCP (Distributed Multi-Threaded Checkpointing) as an alternative checkpointing method [2]. However, the user's application may need to be modified to support DMTCP [2]. The user can also consider purchasing SL2 hours to extend the job runtime to 36 hours [3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the estimated runtime of the job?
- Has the user considered modifying their application to support DMTCP?
- Is the user willing to purchase SL2 hours to extend the job runtime?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your inquiry about checkpointing with Slurm. Unfortunately, the --checkpoint command is no longer supported. You may want to consider using DMTCP as an alternative checkpointing method. If you need help with modifying your application to support DMTCP, please let us know. Alternatively, you can purchase SL2 hours to extend your job runtime to 36 hours.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The checkpoint plugin interface and associated API calls have been removed from Slurm [1].
- The long queue is a higher service level than SL2 and is intended for paying users [3].
- Users can purchase SL2 hours to extend their job runtime to 36 hours [3].

[2] : HPCSSUP-10614
[3] : HPCSSUP-21278
metrics.user_input:
Dear HPC Support team,
could I receive advice on using checkpoints when running a slurm script for AlphaFold 2.3.2?
I have found the following documentation useful:[https://cluster.hpcc.ucr.edu/~jhayes/slurm/16.05.4/checkpoint_blcr.html](https://cluster.hpcc.ucr.edu/~jhayes/slurm/16.05.4/checkpoint_blcr.html)
sbatch
Several options have been added to support checkpoint restart:
- --checkpoint: Specifies the interval between periodic checkpoint of a batch job. By default, the job will have no checkpoints created. Acceptable time formats include "minutes", "minutes:seconds", "hours:minutes:seconds", "days-hours", "days-hours:minutes" and "days-hours:minutes:seconds".
- --checkpoint-dir:Specify the directory when the checkpoint image files of a batch job will be stored. The default value is the current working directory. Checkpoint files will be of the form "<job_id>.ckpt" for jobs and "<job_id>.<step_id>.ckpt" for job steps.
Environment variables are available for all of these options:
SLURM_CHECKPOINT is equivalent to --checkpoint:
- SLURM_CHECKPOINT_DIR is equivalent to --checkpoint-dir
however, the --checkpoint command does not work from a script of from terminal. According to the slurm-rest-23.02 update log (which I believe is the current version of slurm on HPC):
"* The checkpoint plugin interface and all associated API calls have been removed."
Does this mean it is not possible to use checkpoints on HPC?
Thank you for your help,
Lisa

[TICKET_SUMMARY] SLURM job checkpointing

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Does CSD3 Skylake-cpu currently have an available or preferred means to checkpoint and resume jobs? (i.e. save an image of the currently running job, end current slurm job and submit the image as a new slurm job, resuming as if there had been no interruption)
This would be a useful feature, as several members of my group have different applications that have (stochastic) run times exceeding the 12 or 36 hour cutoffs.
While we could modify every application individually to have it save and reload its state, it would be preferable if there was a general way to achieve this.
I can't see anything on this subject listed on the CSD3 documentation.
This might have the features required:
[https://slurm.schedmd.com/checkpoint_blcr.html](https://slurm.schedmd.com/checkpoint_blcr.html)
however it is listed as deprecated (although only as of a more recent version of slurm than CSD3 runs).

Kind Regards
Chris

[SUMMARY] SLURM job checkpointing

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Does CSD3 Skylake-cpu currently have an available or preferred means to checkpoint and resume jobs? (i.e. save an image of the currently running job, end current slurm job and submit the image as a new slurm job, resuming as if there had been no interruption)
This would be a useful feature, as several members of my group have different applications that have (stochastic) run times exceeding the 12 or 36 hour cutoffs.
While we could modify every application individually to have it save and reload its state, it would be preferable if there was a general way to achieve this.
I can't see anything on this subject listed on the CSD3 documentation.
This might have the features required:
[https://slurm.schedmd.com/checkpoint_blcr.html](https://slurm.schedmd.com/checkpoint_blcr.html)
however it is listed as deprecated (although only as of a more recent version of slurm than CSD3 runs).

CLASSIFICATION
Category: GPU Software Build / NVHPC Toolchain
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The build is failing because the user is loading a very old NVHPC toolchain (`nvhpc/21.1`). The linker errors (`crtbegin.o`, `-latomic`, `-lstdc++`) indicate that this compiler stack is not compatible with the current Rocky8/Ampere software environment. The correct fix is to switch to the current Ampere base stack and a newer NVHPC module, then retry the install.

ACTION
1) Purge the current modules.
2) Load the current Ampere software stack:
   `module purge`
   `module load rhel8/ampere/base`
   `module load nvhpc/25.3/gcc/nv5pif7f`
3) Re-activate the virtual environment and rerun the `pip install` command.
4) If the build still fails, request the full updated build log and `module list` output.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Tom,

The failure is caused by the old `nvhpc/21.1` module stack rather than by the `jaxdecomp` package itself.

Please try:
`module purge`
`module load rhel8/ampere/base`
`module load nvhpc/25.3/gcc/nv5pif7f`

Then reactivate your environment and rerun the install.

If it still fails, please send the updated build log and the output of `module list`.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Prefer the current Rocky8/Ampere base stack for Ampere GPU software builds.
- Do not recommend continuing with the deprecated NVHPC 21.1 stack.

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: GPU Software Build / NVHPC Toolchain
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The build is failing because the user is loading a very old NVHPC toolchain (`nvhpc/21.1`). The linker errors (`crtbegin.o`, `-latomic`, `-lstdc++`) indicate that this compiler stack is not compatible with the current Rocky8/Ampere software environment. The correct fix is to switch to the current Ampere base stack and a newer NVHPC module, then retry the install.

ACTION
1) Purge the current modules.
2) Load the current Ampere software stack:
   `module purge`
   `module load rhel8/ampere/base`
   `module load nvhpc/25.3/gcc/nv5pif7f`
3) Re-activate the virtual environment and rerun the `pip install` command.
4) If the build still fails, request the full updated build log and `module list` output.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Tom,

The failure is caused by the old `nvhpc/21.1` module stack rather than by the `jaxdecomp` package itself.

Please try:
`module purge`
`module load rhel8/ampere/base`
`module load nvhpc/25.3/gcc/nv5pif7f`

Then reactivate your environment and rerun the install.

If it still fails, please send the updated build log and the output of `module list`.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Prefer the current Rocky8/Ampere base stack for Ampere GPU software builds.
- Do not recommend continuing with the deprecated NVHPC 21.1 stack.

Hello Daniel,
Can I confirm that you have done the following in your terminal?

```
# Generate run macro run_alphafold3
cat <<EOF> run_alphafold3
#!/bin/bash
SIMAGE=$INSTALL_DIR/alphafold3_3.0.1.sif 
DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/3

singularity exec --env \\
  TF_FORCE_UNIFIED_MEMORY=1,XLA_PYTHON_CLIENT_MEM_FRACTION=4.0,OPENMM_CPU_THREADS=32 \\
  -B \$DATA:/data \\
  -B .:/etc
  --pwd /app/alphafold \\
  --nv \${SIMAGE} \\
  python run_alphafold.py \\
  --db_dir=/data
  "\$@"
EOF

chmod +x run_alphafold3
# Use as is: ./run_alphafold3
# with only alphafold3 option flags not described in the script body above.
```
This should create `run_alphafold3` which should work with the gpus.
Best regards,
Elisabeth

```
# Generate run macro run_alphafold3

#!/bin/bash
SIMAGE=$INSTALL_DIR/alphafold3_3.0.1.sif

  TF_FORCE_UNIFIED_MEMORY=1,XLA_PYTHON_CLIENT_MEM_FRACTION=4.0,OPENMM_CPU_THREADS=32 \\
  -B \$DATA:/data \\
  -B .:/etc
  --pwd /app/alphafold \\
  --nv \${SIMAGE} \\
  python run_alphafold.py \\
  --db_dir=/data
  "\$@"
EOF

chmod +x run_alphafold3
# Use as is: ./run_alphafold3
# with only alphafold3 option flags not described in the script body above.
```

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help running BerkeleyGW 4.0 on Ampere GPU nodes but does not have experience building it there. The best initial support path is to check whether a supported central build/module already exists and, if not, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team rather than relying on the user to complete a complex manual GPU build unaided.

ACTION
1) Confirm the exact version/variant required.
2) Check whether a supported central BerkeleyGW module already exists for Ampere.

4) If no module exists, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bingcan,

We will check whether there is already a supported BerkeleyGW build/module available for Ampere.

If there is, we will send you the exact load commands. If not, we will need the precise version and build requirements so that we can advise on the next step or escalate appropriately.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

BerkeleyGW is built by running a arch.mk file that is architecture dependent, connecting the different required libraries. I’ve included the base file that is the closest to the architecture used on CSD3:
——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————
COMPFLAG = -DINTEL
PARAFLAG = -DMPI -DOMP
MATHFLAG = -DUSESCALAPACK -DUNPACKED -DUSEFFTW3 -DHDF5 -DUSEMR3 # -DUSEPRIMME
1. Only uncomment DEBUGFLAG if you need to develop/debug BerkeleyGW.
2. The output will be much more verbose, and the code will slow down by ~20%.
#DEBUGFLAG = -DDEBUG
#########################################################################
1. NOTE: This arch.mk<[http://arch.mk/](http://arch.mk/)> is used by the testsuite. The compiler flags are #
2. optimized to debug the code and not for code performance.      #
#########################################################################
FCPP  = /usr/bin/cpp -C -nostdinc # -ansi# Intel oneAPI Compiler Options
F90free = mpiifort -free -O3 -qopenmp -g -traceback
LINK  = mpiifort -free -O3 -qopenmp -g -traceback
FOPTS  = -O3
FNOOPTS = $(FOPTS)
MOD_OPT = -module ./
INCFLAG = -I# Intel oneAPI C Compiler Options
CC_COMP = mpiicpc -qopenmp
C_COMP  = mpiicc -qopenmp
C_LINK  = mpiicc -qopenmp
C_OPTS  = -O3
C_DEBUGFLAG =REMOVE = /bin/rm -f# Math Libraries
#MKLROOT = /opt/intel/oneapi/mkl/latest
FFTWLIB   = $(MKLROOT)/lib/intel64/libmkl_scalapack_lp64.a Wl,-start-group $(MKLROOT)/lib/intel64/libmkl_intel_lp64.a \

HDF5LIB   = $(HDF5_LDIR)/libhdf5hl_fortran.a \
        $(HDF5_LDIR)/libhdf5_hl.a \
        $(HDF5_LDIR)/libhdf5_fortran.a \
        $(HDF5_LDIR)/libhdf5.a -lz -ldl
HDF5INCLUDE = /usr/local/Cluster-Apps/hdf5/mpi/intel/2019.3/1.10.5/include

——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————

——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————

#DEBUGFLAG = -DDEBUG
#########################################################################
1. NOTE: This arch.mk is used by a buildslave. The compiler flags are  #
1. optimized to debug the code and not for code performance.           #
#########################################################################
FCPP    = cpp -C -nostdinc -nostdinc++
#F90free = /usr/bin/mpif90 -ffree-form -ffree-line-length-none -Wall
F90free = /usr/bin/mpif90 -ffree-form -ffree-line-length-none -fbounds-check -Wall -pedantic-errors -std=gnu
#F90free = /usr/bin/mpif90 -ffree-form -ffree-line-length-none -fbounds-check -fbacktrace -ffpe-trap=invalid,zero,overflow

checking for Fortran flag to compile .f90 files... unknown
configure: error: Fortran could not compile .f90 files
Should I change the MPIF90 option to something else?
Best,
Kem
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0007 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Ah yes, `mpifort` is not a wrapper provided by Intel MPI. Unfortunately there is no consensus on what fortran compilers should be called - fort, f90, fc are all used. In this case you can use mpif90 rather than mpifort.
Best wishes,
Jeffrey.
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0008 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Ok, I think I compiled it succesfully and it works now. Thanks, Jeffrey.
Best,
Kem
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0009 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
No problem, good luck with your project!
Best wishes,
Jeffrey.
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

The user assumed they needed `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` for DAWN XPU work. On DAWN, the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes, so the supported first step is to use the provided module stack rather than trying to install system packages with `sudo`.

ACTION
1) Explain that the node drivers should already be present.
2) Ask whether they are using a container or virtual environment.
3) Suggest loading the provided module:
   `module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`
4) If they still prefer a user-space install, confirm they can do that in their own environment rather than system-wide.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Jiangnan,

You should not need to use `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` on DAWN, because the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes.

Please first try:
`module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`

If you are building inside a container or a virtual environment, please let us know which approach you are using so we can advise more precisely.

Best regards,
HPC Support

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support

The error message indicates that the shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate' [2]. This issue can be resolved by adding the necessary commands to the submission script to configure the shell and activate the Conda environment [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user should add the following lines to their submission script:
1. `module load miniconda/3` to load the Miniconda module [2].
2. `source ~/.bashrc` to configure the shell [2].
3. `conda activate <env_name>` to activate the Conda environment [1][2].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

metrics.user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support

The user is experiencing issues with activating a Conda environment in a SLURM submission script [1][2]. The error message indicates that the shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate' [2]. This issue can be resolved by adding the necessary commands to the submission script to configure the shell and activate the Conda environment [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user should add the following lines to their submission script:
1. `module load miniconda/3` to load the Miniconda module [2].
2. `source ~/.bashrc` to configure the shell [2].
3. `conda activate <env_name>` to activate the Conda environment [1][2].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

I have added the necessary lines to my submission script and the issue is now resolved. The Conda environment is activating correctly and my job is running without errors.
Thank you for your assistance.
Best regards,
[User]

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The user should ensure that they have the necessary permissions to load modules and activate Conda environments [1][2].
- The user should also ensure that they are using the correct version of Conda and the correct environment name [1][2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-16898
[2] : HPCSSUP-52206

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Platform Incident / Filesystem Performance
Technical Action Required: Yes (investigate possible service-side performance issue)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user’s slow Python setup and `import torch` freezes could reflect either a problem in the user environment or a broader shared-filesystem or service issue. Because the slowdown affects multiple environment-creation methods and both home and `hpc-work`, the first response should check for any active service-side problem before focusing only on the user’s setup.

ACTION
1) Check whether there is a broader service issue affecting Python environment creation or shared filesystems.
2) Ask the user to confirm the login node and working path they are using.
3) If no active incident is found, collect the exact commands and timings needed to separate user-environment issues from platform performance issues.
4) Ask the user to retry once those checks have been completed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stelios,

The symptoms you describe may indicate a broader platform performance issue rather than only a mistake in your Python environment setup.

We are checking this on our side. In the meantime, please confirm which login node and working path you are using so that we can investigate further if needed.

Best regards,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Python / Conda Environment Setup [1][2]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Escalation Required: Possibly [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing slow Python execution and Conda environment setup on CSD3, which has been reported in previous tickets [1][2][3][4][5]. The issue may be related to the filesystem or network I/O, as suggested in ticket [1]. The user has tried using their own installation of micromamba, miniconda3, and the pre-installed miniconda3, but the problem persists.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Investigate the filesystem and network I/O to determine if there are any issues that could be causing the slow execution [1].
2. Check if the problem is specific to the user's environment or if it's a more general issue affecting multiple users [1][4].
3. Consider escalating the issue to the relevant team for further investigation and resolution [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about your environment setup and the exact commands you're running?
- Have you tried running your Python scripts on a different node or cluster to see if the issue is specific to one machine?
- Are there any error messages or output that could help us diagnose the issue?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting the issue with slow Python execution and Conda environment setup on CSD3. We're currently investigating the issue and will update you once we have more information. In the meantime, please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Be cautious when troubleshooting Conda environments to avoid causing further issues or conflicts [2].
- Ensure that all investigations and actions are conducted in accordance with CSD3 policies and procedures [1][2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-98717
[2] : HPCSSUP-88199
[3] : HPCSSUP-88200
[4] : HPCSSUP-97213
[5] : HPCSSUP-91091
metrics.user_input:
Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Slow Conda environment setup and activation on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC Support Team,
I am a PhD student in the Engineering Department, division F, have been experiencing some significant delays with conda environment setup and activation on CSD3, and am attaching details below which I hope can be helpful in diagnosing the issue.
1. Environment build failures
I had to make three attempts to build a conda environment with PyTorch 2.8.0 and pyannote.audio 4.0.3. The first two jobs timed out despite using very little CPU or memory. The bottleneck appears to be network I/O when downloading packages. Here are the details:
Job ID
State
Wall-clock
CPU Used
Memory Used
21592679
TIMEOUT (killed during "Verifying transaction")
02:00:11
00:01:22 (0.28%)
357 MB (2.18%)
21596158
TIMEOUT (killed during "Installing pip dependencies")
04:00:08
00:02:00 (0.21%)
508 MB (3.10%)
21629759
COMPLETED
00:07:36
00:01:17 (3.38%)
278 MB (1.70%)
The third job completed quickly because the packages were already cached from the previous two attempts.
2. Conda activation and script runtime speed
Since last Thursday, activating conda environment has been notably slow. I had to remove the conda initialisation lines from my ~/.bashrc file in order to access the login node. I also tried requesting an interactive session and a CPU cluster node, but it could take 10–20 minutes to activate a conda environment. Things have been better today (around 3–5 minutes), but I thought it was still worth reporting in case the issue recurs.
Additionally, I have a testing script for the conda environment that finishes running in under 20 seconds (including initialisation time) on local machine, but takes 8–9 minutes to run on the login node or an interactive CPU session.
I am CC-ing colleagues in my team and @Jinghong Chen<[jc2124@cam.ac.uk](mailto:jc2124@cam.ac.uk)> and @Guangyu Yang<[gy266@cam.ac.uk](mailto:gy266@cam.ac.uk)>, whose team is experiencing similar conda-related slowness on the GPU cluster as well: a programme that is expected to finish in half an hour is taking over 6 hours to run.
I was wondering whether the slowness related to network I/O and conda is expected, and whether there is anything we are missing?
Many thanks and I look forward to hearing from you!
Best regards,
Shilin

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Cannot set-up conda environment on CSD3 HPC

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear CSD3 team,
I have encountered a hurdle when trying to set-up and run computations on your cluster for the first time.
I am unable to successfully set-up my Conda environment with the packages that I require to run my Python script. I have been working to resolve this issue for multiple days now but at no avail.
Initially, I started by trying to re-create the Conda environment that I currently work on within a different  compute cluster. When this did not work, I simply tried installing one package at a time, but would still encounter the same issue regardless of how simple I tried to make the process.
The error I keep encountering (many times, sometimes in slightly differing error messages) is that the environment is inconsistent and it fails to resolve. Here are the packages that I need to install to allow my code to run:
bctpy – Brain Connectivity Toolbox (Python version; installable via pip)
*
h5py
*
matplotlib
*
networkx
*
numpy
*
pandas
*
plotly
*
scikit-learn
*
scipy
*
seaborn
*
torch
*
umap-learn
I have tried different solutions including updating conda (was not able to do this, same issue) and installing mamba (was not able to do this, same issue). I am not very knowledgeable about these things - but I have been able to work with conda successfully on another compute cluster, no issue.
I would very much appreciate any help that you could give me. I am available at any time to provide any more information, or to work through this through videocall/screenshare if this is something that you do.
Thanks and best wishes,
El

Dear HPC team,
I'm trying to activate my Conda environment for course work (`MLMI2`)
and the conda environment simply doesn't load (`source /rds/project/rds-xyBFuSj0hm0/MLMI2.M2025/miniconda3/bin/activate`).

Trying to run any python files afterwards takes ages (waiting > 3 mins with no result for a script that used to run < 10s).
Also: connecting to my github account and fetching updates I've written on my local machine takes significantly longer than before.
Do you have an idea what this might be caused by? This has started today, so it might be connected with the throttling ([https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/data_centre_upgrade.html#key-dates](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/data_centre_upgrade.html#key-dates))?
Yesterday everything worked fine.
This affects all multiple machines I've provisioned (in interactive sessions).
Thank you in advance for your support!
- Konstantin Sturtzkopf

I also tried requesting an interactive session and a CPU cluster node, but it could take 10–20 minutes to activate a conda environment. Things have been better today (around 3–5 minutes), but I thought it was still worth reporting in case the issue recurs.
Additionally, I have a testing script for the conda environment that finishes running in under 20 seconds (including initialisation time) on local machine, but takes 8–9 minutes to run on the login node or an interactive CPU session.
I am CC-ing colleagues in my team and @Jinghong Chen<[jc2124@cam.ac.uk](mailto:jc2124@cam.ac.uk)> and @Guangyu Yang<[gy266@cam.ac.uk](mailto:gy266@cam.ac.uk)>, whose team is experiencing similar conda-related slowness on the GPU cluster as well: a programme that is expected to finish in half an hour is taking over 6 hours to run.
I was wondering whether the slowness related to network I/O and conda is expected, and whether there is anything we are missing?
Many thanks and I look forward to hearing from you!
Best regards,
Shilin

We’re currently investigating an ongoing issue of which your reported problem is a part of. I will update you once I know more.

Elisabeth Reeve
HPC Support

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hello Shilin,
Are you still experiencing these issues?
Best regards,
Elisabeth

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Environment Performance / Shared Storage
Technical Action Required: No (performance guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeing slow Python imports from a virtual environment stored on `hpc-work`. A likely explanation is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem rather than a Python bug, but support should first confirm there is no known wider incident before treating it as normal shared-storage behaviour.

ACTION
1) Explain that `hpc-work` is a shared RDS filesystem and can slow down when heavily loaded.
2) Check whether any cluster-wide incident is currently known.
3) Advise the user that environments on shared storage may show intermittent latency.
4) If the slowdown becomes persistent or widespread, request timings and node details for escalation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

A likely cause is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem, since your virtual environment is stored in `hpc-work`, but we would first want to rule out any broader service issue.

If no wider incident is present, imports can sometimes be slower when the shared storage is under heavy load.

If this becomes persistent or starts affecting many commands consistently, please send timings and the exact node and location you are testing from and we can investigate further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.user_input:
Hi,
I am having an issue when debugging my Python code on the gfx nodes: when I try to run simple scripts which should finish in less than 1 second, they sometimes take 10s of seconds or even minutes in some cases to run.
When interrupting one of these scripts (with CTRL-C) the python traceback shows that the process was terminated when trying to import various modules.
The below terminal output from a very simple script where only a single module is imported is shown below:
[cid:a6653bb8-3762-4b4a-9dc4-5d29b74c798f]
As you can see, I ran the exact same command 3 times, with one of them taking substantially longer than the other two.
This issue seems to happen when more than a few minutes has passed since I last ran a script and is starting to become a real pain to my workflow as I when I make changes to code and want to test them I sometimes have to wait minutes for the script to run when it should take ~1 second.
To give some extra details as to my setup; I am using uv package manager with python 3.12.12. UV sets up a python virtual environment (venv) and installs dependencies there as is best practise.
If I had to guess as to what is going on, it seems to be some kind of file system latency where the venv files are normally in some kind of cache so are fast but after a while has passed they are removed and must be retrieved from somewhere much slower. Note that all of the python files and modules are stored in my RDS hpc-work folder.
Would you be able to look into this please? Perhaps there is some quirk of/issue with the lustre filesystem that is causing this?
Many thanks,
Leo

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The access failure is unlikely to be explained by a separate service-specific password, especially since the user can log in elsewhere successfully. The most likely next steps are to check whether there is an access-control/group-membership problem and to confirm that the user has generated the correct MFA token for RCS/CSD3 access via `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

ACTION
1) Check whether the account has the correct storage/access-group membership.
2) Instruct the user to create the correct TOTP via:
   `ssh <crsid>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
3) Share the MFA documentation.
4) Remind the user of the normal login path and storage path once access has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Christina,

You should not need a separate password for this service. We will check your storage access permissions on our side.

You will also need to create the MFA token that works with RCS/CSD3 access. Please run:
`ssh cez22@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the on-screen instructions. Guidance is here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk

Best wishes,
HPC Support

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Issues [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: No [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing access issues with their RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski) using FileZilla, despite successfully logging in to the HPC login web page and setting up MFA/TOTP for CSD3 [1]. The user is entering their password and TOTP verification code, but is being denied access [1]. 

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, the user should try accessing the RCS storage using the correct TOTP code, labelled as “CSD3:SSH” [3]. If the user does not have this code, or if it is not working, the MFA can be reset [3]. The user should also ensure that they are using the correct server address (rcs.uis.cam.ac.uk) and protocol (SFTP) [1][2]. 

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm that you are using the correct TOTP code, labelled as “CSD3:SSH”?
- Have you tried accessing the RCS storage using a different method, such as the command line?
- Are you using the correct server address and protocol?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To access your RCS storage, please ensure that you are using the correct TOTP code, labelled as “CSD3:SSH”. If you do not have this code, or if it is not working, please let us know and we can reset the MFA for you. You can find more information on accessing RCS storage in the user documentation: https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rcs/index.html [3].
Please get back in touch if you have any further issues.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The user should ensure that they are using the correct TOTP code, labelled as “CSD3:SSH”, to avoid any security issues [3].
- The user should also ensure that they are using the correct server address and protocol to avoid any access issues [1][2].

[2] : HPCSSUP-51042
[3] : HPCSSUP-60259

You will be asked to enter your Raven password and if login is successful presented with QR code. The code needs to be scanned with authenticator app on your phone which should then produce 6 digit number that changes every 30 seconds. Greg

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Reset / SSH Re-enrolment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has lost access to the TOTP setup previously generated for cluster SSH login and needs the standard cluster MFA reset/re-enrolment path. After reset, they should SSH to `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk` again to obtain a new QR code for cluster login.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the user can transfer the existing authenticator setup from the old device; if not, follow the standard cluster MFA reset workflow.
2) Reset the user’s cluster MFA using the normal support process.
3) Instruct them to run:
   `ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
   using their Cambridge password to obtain a new QR code.
4) Clarify that the web interface may prompt separately if web MFA also needs re-enrolment.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

We can reset the MFA for your cluster account and then you can re-enrol it.

Once the reset has been completed, please run:
`ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the prompts to scan the new QR code into your authenticator app.

Best regards,
HPC Support

When I try to connect to the login node (login.hpc.cam.ac.uk) through ssh I am being requested to provide the TOTP Verification Code. Unfortunately, I did not retain the QR code when connecting for the first time to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Would it be possible to reset this multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk QR code so I can scan it with Google Authenticator to set the login for my account?
